mnemonics.title: DDoS Attack Types
mnemonics.content: VIP: Volumetric (UDP/ICMP floods), Protocol (SYN/Ping of Death/Smurf), Application layer (HTTP/Slowloris)

mnemonics.title: DDoS Symptoms
mnemonics.content: SSSD: Slow performance, Service unavailability, Spam increase, Sudden traffic from unusual locations

acronyms.usage: Attack from single source to make service unavailable
Attack from multiple sources (botnets) to overwhelm target
Network of compromised computers controlled by attacker
Distributed servers that absorb DDoS traffic
Protocol used in Ping of Death and Smurf attacks
First step in TCP three-way handshake exploited in SYN floods
Application layer attack that holds connections open like Slowloris

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a restaurant where people constantly sit at tables (connections) and order food (SYN), but never eat or leave. Eventually, all tables are occupied and legitimate customers cannot be served.
Think of a botnet as a zombie army - many compromised computers (zombies) following one commander's (botmaster) orders to attack a single target.
Like using a microphone and speaker system - a small voice (small request) is amplified into a huge sound (large response) directed at the victim.
Imagine many people entering a store, browsing for hours without buying anything, blocking legitimate customers from getting service - that's how Slowloris works.

analogies.analogy: DoS is like one person blocking your door, while DDoS is like a hundred people from different locations blocking all entrances to your building simultaneously.
Like a traffic jam caused by thousands of cars entering a small road at the same time - the road (bandwidth) is completely saturated.
Like a zombie apocalypse where thousands of computers are 'infected' and controlled by one 'master zombie' to attack the same target.
Like having multiple branches of a bank across a city - if one branch is attacked, customers can be redirected to other branches, protecting the main vault.
